text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025

children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

29E.1	The United Nations Office at Geneva was established in 1947 pursuant to General Assembly resolution 24 (I) and successive resolutions of the Assembly. The Office is responsible for providing administrative and support services to entities in Geneva and their field offices, and to other organizations of the United Nations common system, in accordance with policies, procedures and practices established by the Under-Secretary-General for Management Strategy, Policy and Compliance, the Under-Secretary-General for Operational Support and the Assistant Secretary-General/Chief Information Technology Officer. The Office is also responsible for providing support services to the intergovernmental processes of the Organization in line with its role as a major conference centre for international diplomacy. This includes providing technical conference support services as part of the conferencing ecosystem, which enables the work of Member States and client entities.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

29E.2	In compliance with General Assembly resolution 2 (I), the Office will offer administrative and support services in English and French, including direction, management and coordination in the areas of human resources, financial and budgetary resources, information and communications technology (ICT), central support services, and library and archives services. The Office, as property manager for the United Nations premises, which accommodate nearly 70 tenants, will continue to play a central role in supporting United Nations meetings and events through audiovisual and ICT services, as well as the maintenance of conference rooms and equipment. The Office will also implement policy and other functions, as determined by the Under-Secretaries-General referred to in paragraph 29E.1 above.

29E.3	The Office will provide financial, budgetary and accounting services, as well as guidance and advice, in accordance with the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations and relevant standards. In addition, the Office will provide human resources services, including staff development and learning, career support and coaching, and medico-administrative occupational safety and health, and carry out staff mental health and well-being activities, as well as provide guidance and advice in accordance with the Organization’s human resources policies, rules and regulations. The Office will provide procurement, commercial activities, transportation, travel and other central support services for the Secretariat and the specialized agencies. It will also provide regional administrative services, as well as library, records, archives and knowledge services, in support of the implementation of mandated programmes and activities of the Secretariat, including to resident coordinator offices. The Office will further ensure the business continuity of administrative support and guidance for other entities in Geneva in emergency cases.

29E.4	Renovation works at the historic buildings of the Palais des Nations under the strategic heritage plan will continue throughout 2027, with substantial completion expected during the year and residual works continuing into 2028. The Office will strengthen systems supporting building operations, maintenance and energy management to reduce energy consumption, optimize asset performance and lower life-cycle costs while improving reliability and sustainability.

29E.5	Operational efficiency, cost control and client support will remain priorities in 2027. Quarterly client board meetings will continue to promote transparent service delivery, supported by regular reporting on performance indicators covering more than 20 services. Client engagement will be further strengthened through dedicated functional client forums, introduced in 2026, which provide structured opportunities for in-depth dialogue between the Office’s functional leads and subject matter experts of client entities on operational issues, performance trends and service improvements. The Office will continue to provide operational support through its Client Support Centre, delivering tier 2-level support and handling approximately 20,000 client visits and 12,000 client calls annually. In response to growing demand and increasing technical complexity related to delivering side events, cultural events and activities, client support will also be provided through the Event Support Centre, launched in January 2026. Dedicated support will also continue for client entities relocating to or from the Palais des Nations. Operational efficiency will be further strengthened through faster, more reliable network services for clients. All efforts will be supported by a targeted communications strategy informed by annual client entity and end-user surveys.

29E.6	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, the Office will continue to collaborate with other Geneva-based United Nations system organizations and international governmental organizations, coordinating the Common Procurement Activities Group. The Office will continue to own and administer global and regional United Nations contracts, conduct annual daily subsistence allowance surveys, coordinate cost-of-living surveys, conduct place-to-place surveys and monitor the consumer price index at Geneva. The Office will also work closely with facilities managers of international organizations in Geneva to strengthen coordination on policies and procedures. In addition, the Office will collaborate with other United Nations libraries to digitize official United Nations documents and further streamline library activities by managing the common library management system and catalogue for United Nations Headquarters, the Economic Commission for Latin America and the Caribbean, the Economic Commission for Africa and the Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific.

29E.7	With regard to external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the planning assumption that client offices will have sufficient extrabudgetary resources to pay for services provided by the Office, when applicable.

29E.8	The Office integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. In 2027, it will contribute to improving gender equality in supported offices, in line with the Secretary-General’s system-wide strategy on gender parity, through consultations and briefings to hiring managers and human resources focal points on the impact of selection decisions, including advice on targeted outreach and the promotion of United Nations career opportunities.

29E.9	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the Office will continue to implement its disability inclusion action plan and support the strategic heritage plan in its efforts to incorporate accessibility features in renovation works.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 initiative

29E.10	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. In particular, the tables of deliverables reflect updated planned deliverables for 2026, in line with the approved programme budget for 2026. In addition, in the light of the consolidation of payroll processing into a single global team, the Office will cease payroll operations, as reflected in the updated table of deliverables of subprogramme 1. In addition, in the context of continuing organizational changes, the Office will support clients, managers and staff throughout any future transitions, as reflected in result 3 under subprogramme 2.

29E.11	The Office will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, among others, any decisions concerning proposals relating to the establishment of a common administrative platform in Geneva, as part of the next phase of consolidation and streamlining of support services and structures across the Secretariat.
Legislative mandates
29E.12	The list below, which was reviewed in the preparation of the proposed programme budget, provides all mandates entrusted to the programme.

children.text: Evaluation activities
29E.14	An evaluation conducted by the Office on optimized warehouse storage space for its clients, completed in 2025, has guided the proposed programme plan for 2027.

29E.15	In response to the results of the evaluation referenced above, the Office incorporated recommendations and lessons learned to inform its work referenced in paragraph 29E.44 under subprogramme 3 to optimize the use of space and improve logistical services and support for client entities.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Programme planning, finance and budget
Objective
29E.16	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure sound, effective and efficient financial management by client offices.
Strategy
29E.17	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Monitor budget implementation, report budget and financial information and conduct financial management and accounts control by coordinating with Headquarters and providing timely advice to client entities regarding a broad range of financial services, spanning from payments and disbursements services to accounting, medical insurance, and trust funds and budget management;
(b)	Provide assistance, guidance and training to programme managers and administrative units;

(c)	Perform financial and administrative procedures, including internal financial controls and monitoring strict compliance with the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations and the financial policies of the United Nations;

(d)	Identify ways to achieve a faster and more efficient client response, including through digital transformation and innovative tools in line with the Secretary-General’s strategy on new technologies;
(e)	Monitor the implementation of oversight body recommendations;
(f)	Monitor and report on enterprise risk management.
29E.18	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in improved integrity of financial data and enhanced overall management of programme budget and extrabudgetary resources.
Programme performance in 2025
Improved timeliness of vendor payments

29E.19	In 2025, the subprogramme introduced a system enhancement to streamline vendor invoice payments by automating the verification of invoices attached to the service entry sheet in Umoja. Where an invoice is present, payment can be initiated immediately, eliminating the need for separate client requests through iNeed. The new workflow reduced processing time and administrative efforts for clients and the subprogramme, strengthened controls and supported timely vendor payments.
29E.20	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29E.2).
Table 29E.2
Performance measure

table_content.text: Payment was initiated immediately for approximately one third of payments

children.text: Result 3: clients gain clearer insight into service performance through quarterly reporting – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29E.25	The subprogramme is responsible for providing clients with a service-level agreement containing performance indicators. In its 2025 audit of the arrangements for provision of administrative services by the United Nations Office at Geneva, the Office of Internal Oversight Services (OIOS) found that the performance indicators included in the Office’s 2025 service-level agreements were primarily service standards and did not meet the criteria of measurable performance indicators. In response to the audit recommendation, the subprogramme worked with service lines to identify 24 client-focused performance indicators, which were incorporated into the 2026 service-level agreements and shared with clients.
Lessons learned and planned change

29E.26	The lesson for the subprogramme was that client satisfaction and service delivery can be strengthened through regular reporting on performance results and structured discussion of those results with clients. In applying this lesson, the subprogramme will establish a central reporting capacity for performance indicators across all subprogrammes and produce service-line reports for discussion at quarterly client board meetings. Performance results will also be examined in greater detail through service-level client forums for each subprogramme, providing structured opportunities for dialogue on service delivery and operational issues. Based on client feedback, the performance indicators and reporting approach will be further refined to enhance their relevance and to support continuous improvement in service delivery, including as part of the implementation of a common administrative platform in Geneva.
29E.27	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29E.5).

children.text: Deliverables
29E.28	Table 29E.6 lists all deliverables of the subprogramme.
Table 29E.6
Subprogramme 1: deliverables for 2027, by category and subcategory

table_content.text: Administration: financial management, risk management and internal control, and preparation and implementation of resource proposals; management of more than 700 posts; financial reporting and accounting; cash and revenue management; management of service arrangements with 50 client entities across 120 service lines; processing of more than 300,000 payments and disbursements annually; guidance and advice on all budgetary and financial matters; provision of medical insurance to more than 36,000 staff, retirees and dependants; processing of more than 490,000 medical invoices annually; administration of compensation claims; and delivery of quarterly client board meetings

children.text: Subprogramme 2 – Human resources management
Objective
29E.29	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure an effective, adaptable and resilient workforce that upholds the highest standards of efficiency, competence and integrity.
Strategy
29E.30	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide expert support to clients to attract, recruit and retain talent, including applicants from unrepresented and underrepresented countries, by leveraging efficient workforce planning and internal human resources analytics capabilities and engaging in outreach activities such as career fairs;
(b)	Strengthen tailored advisory services and adopt a cross-functional approach to case management, and promote staff well-being and an inclusive work culture;

(c)	Provide learning and development activities, such as training sessions and briefings on performance management, innovation and navigation of workplace changes, as well as through language programmes to promote multilingualism;
(d)	Promote a harmonious workplace with zero tolerance for prohibited conduct.
29E.31	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Improved client experience, satisfaction and decision-making, on the basis of enhanced business insight using human resources data;
(b)	Staff successfully navigating workplace changes, embracing innovation and adopting new ways of working;

(c)	Increased awareness of the mechanisms to address prohibited conduct and of the responsibility of individual staff and management to foster a harmonious, inclusive and psychologically safe work environment;
(d)	A widened pool of young and geographically diverse applicants.
Programme performance in 2025
Staff support for career transition and early separation

29E.32	During implementation of the global early separation programme launched by the Secretary-General, the subprogramme supported staff, managers and client entities by advising individuals on their entitlements and separation procedures, and guiding managers in its application.

29E.33	The subprogramme responded to a high volume of advisory cases in a timely and accurate manner, fostering trust and transparency among staff, managers and clients. The subprogramme also delivered targeted career transition sessions and collaborated with Geneva-based United Nations common system organizations and external partners, including the European Union. In coordination with host country authorities, dedicated briefings were organized to support staff on separation-related matters, including residency permits, post-service arrangements and access to the Swiss labour market. These initiatives received positive feedback and helped mitigate the impact of organizational change.
29E.34	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29E.7).
Table 29E.7
Performance measure

table_content.text: Over 100 staff affected by early separation and their managers were able to navigate separation procedures and transition arrangements with clarity

Figure 29E.I
Performance measure: percentage of interns from unrepresented and underrepresented countries (annual)
Result 3: improved workforce support and organizational readiness through transitions – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29E.39	In the context of continued organizational changes, client entities have faced increasing complexity when navigating human resource processes. To respond to clients’ needs, among other initiatives, the subprogramme designated human resource business partners for each client entity to support collaboration and responsiveness.
Lessons learned and planned change

29E.40	The lesson for the subprogramme was that structured advisory services and early involvement of human resource business partners effectively support mandate delivery and staff well-being during periods of transition. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will establish dedicated human resources business advisory services to provide structured support to clients, managers and staff. This will include guidance on change management, clarification of roles and responsibilities, and coordinated engagement with global human resources service providers.
29E.41	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29E.9).
Table 29E.9

children.text: Deliverables
29E.42	Table 29E.10 lists all deliverables of the subprogramme.
Table 29E.10
Subprogramme 2: deliverables for 2027, by category and subcategory

table_content.text: Outreach programmes, special events and information materials: job fairs and career presentations to identify prospective candidates from unrepresented and underrepresented Member States; and knowledge-sharing in the area of human resources and staff health and well-being.

Administration: talent management and job classification; administration of approximately 3,150 staff contracts; advice and guidance on human resources policies, including on the Staff Regulations and Rules and staff selection system; 500 learning activities, including training on organizational and managerial competencies, substantive and technical skills, language and communications, and career development for 13,000 staff members and others annually; administration of assessment tests; staff counselling services and psychological support; conduct of staff surveys, including on cost of living and daily subsistence allowance; approximately 8,400 host country residence permits obtained in liaison with local authorities; occupational health and medico-administrative services for approximately 15,000 client staff in Geneva and field offices, medical consultations and advice, medical emergency preparedness, health and well-being campaigns; and learning, career development and performance management.

Internal justice and oversight: human resources-related legal services provided to the Office; represent the Secretary-General before the United Nations Dispute Tribunal; act as Office legal adviser in human resources matters, including in cases of alleged misconduct; support management in reviewing human resources-related administrative decisions; negotiate settlement agreements on behalf of the Secretariat and work with the United Nations Ombudsman and Mediation Services; act as conduct and discipline focal point for the Office; and provide regular briefings for managers on best practices and lessons learned from current legal jurisprudence to increase awareness and compliance.

children.text: Subprogramme 3 – Support services
Objective

29E.43	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure the efficient, effective and environmentally responsible functioning of the United Nations Office at Geneva with regard to premises, office and conference facilities management; asset management; travel and transportation; mail and pouch services; procurement; immunities and privileges; and commercial activities.
Strategy
29E.44	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide services in the areas of facilities management, security infrastructure maintenance, assistance with conference activities, special events, project management, and property and space management;
(b)	Equip, operate and maintain meeting and conference rooms with advanced technologies, infrastructure and equipment;

(c)	Provide procurement services, including collaborative procurement activities with other United Nations organizations and agencies based in Geneva and regionally in Europe through the Common Procurement Activities Group;

(d)	Provide services in the areas of contract management, travel, administration of diplomatic privileges, visas, United Nations laissez-passer and travel documents, transportation, mail and pouch services, and warehousing;

(e)	Support the implementation of the strategic heritage plan, ensuring that solutions are in place to minimize the impact of any potential disruptions during renovation of the historic buildings of the Palais des Nations and operation of building H.
29E.45	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Optimized and safe use of space and the provision of high-quality logistical services and support to client entities;
(b)	Modern, high-quality conference support services, including the availability of conference rooms equipped for multilingualism;
(c)	Clients’ procurement needs being met.
Programme performance in 2025
Assembly Hall audiovisual systems help deliver large-scale intergovernmental meetings and events

29E.46	The Assembly Hall, the largest conference room in the Palais des Nations, is used for major conferences, commemorations, concerts and award ceremonies. It underwent extensive renovation under the strategic heritage plan from June 2021 to April 2025, limiting the ability to hold large-scale events.

29E.47	The subprogramme played a central role in modernizing the Hall, upgrading audiovisual facilities while preserving the building’s heritage and ensuring the continuity of high-quality conference services. This was achieved through the dismantling, overhauling, upgrading, reinstallation and reprogramming of the entire conference system, including 681 seats with microphones, 1,032 seats with listening devices, 14 interpretation booths and 7 cameras. In addition, two large screens and an image capture system were installed, an operator control room developed and the sound system fully replaced. The renovation significantly enhanced service reliability and meeting delivery, facilitating live broadcasting and improved accessibility for delegates and participants. Close cooperation between the strategic heritage plan and the subprogramme enabled the Office to host 10 large-scale events.
29E.48	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29E.11).
Table 29E.11
Performance measure

table_content.text:
Large-scale events included, among others:
(a)	Intergovernmental negotiating committee to develop an international legally binding instrument on plastic pollution, including in the marine environment, with 2,600 participants from 183 countries
(b)	Sixteenth session of the United Nations Conference on Trade and Development
(c)	Seventy-sixth session of the Executive Committee of the Programme of the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees
(d)	Fiftieth session of the Working Group on the Universal Periodic Review of the Human Rights Council
(e)	Sixth World Conference of Speakers of Parliament
(f)	22nd Meeting of the States Parties to the Convention on the Prohibition of the Use, Stockpiling, Production and Transfer of Anti-Personnel Mines and on Their Destruction

children.text: Result 3: improved energy management across the Palais des Nations – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29E.53	The Office has strengthened its approach to energy management to better control operating costs, reduce waste and improve energy efficiency in the context of constrained resources. Activities have included adopting action plans for energy management, waste management and biodiversity; conducting energy and waste audits; and implementing cost-free energy and waste reduction measures.
Lessons learned and planned change

29E.54	The lesson for the subprogramme was that high levels of energy and environmental performance are best achieved through an integrated, organization-wide management framework rather than ad hoc or asset-specific measures. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will lead and coordinate the implementation of an energy management system to support more efficient operations across the Palais des Nations. The system will enable structured monitoring and management of energy use, reduce inefficiencies and support cost avoidance as renovated buildings return to service. Improvements related to environmental impact implemented in 2026 will be followed by the phased roll-out of the energy management system in renovated buildings in 2027. These efforts will be aligned with relevant United Nations policies and internationally recognized standards, including International Organization for Standardization (ISO) 14001 and 50001, and will support reliable operations under resource constraints.
29E.55	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29E.14).

children.text: Subprogramme 4 – Information and communications technology operations
Objective

29E.57	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance the economic, efficient and effective implementation of substantive programmes through technologies, including a coherent enterprise ICT system and enterprise architecture; access to ICT-related technological innovations; and a stable, secure and standardized technology platform that enables the Organization to run its operations cost-effectively.
Strategy
29E.58	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Manage a portfolio of end-to-end hardware and software solutions and implement new technologies based on business objectives and prioritizing optimization;

(b)	Build resilient technological capacity to meet objectives by migrating to cost-effective sourcing options, exploiting economies of scale, adopting proven platform-agnostic technologies to minimize provider dependency and discontinuing legacy systems;

(c)	Manage the use of resources by engaging and leading the ICT community and entities of the United Nations system in Geneva and other offices based in Europe for which the Office is service provider in the definition, adoption and deployment of ICT governance policies and procedures, overseeing and supporting ICT strategy implementation and providing secure, standardized ICT services.
29E.59	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	A safe and reliable ICT environment for all United Nations Secretariat entities in Geneva;
(b)	More timely and better informed administrative and management decision-making facilitated by information technology solutions;
(c)	Cost-effective services and solutions that optimize the use of resources for mandate delivery.
Programme performance in 2025
Clients achieve more efficient fixed phone line use

29E.60	Reducing the number of fixed phone lines enables clients to eliminate unnecessary costs and optimize resources. In 2025, the subprogramme supported informed decision-making by providing clients with tailored reports identifying active, unused and redundant fixed phone lines, by reconciling data from multiple sources to compensate for the absence of predefined reporting tools. As a result, client entities were able to reduce the number of fixed phone lines by 66 per cent between 2023 and 2025.
29E.61	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29E.II).
Figure 29E.II
Performance measure: number of fixed phone lines in use at the Palais des Nations (annual)

children.text: Result 3: faster, more reliable network services for clients – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29E.66	Reliable network services underpin the Office’s operations, including meetings, conferences and digital collaboration. During 2025, the subprogramme assessed options for modernizing the network infrastructure, including by replacing legacy core network switches, upgrading wireless access points, transitioning users from wired to high-performance wireless connectivity and decommissioning redundant switches to improve reliability, capacity and cost efficiency.
Lessons learned and planned change

29E.67	The lesson for the subprogramme was that improvements to network resilience and reliability can be achieved by concentrating resources on fewer, standardized network solutions, reducing dependency on legacy configurations and concentrating resources on scalable, high-impact solutions. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will replace wireless access points, optimize wireless coverage, transition users from wired to wireless connectivity and decommission redundant switches. These actions will support faster, more reliable network services for clients and strengthen the resilience of ICT operations.
29E.68	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29E.18).

children.text: Subprogramme 5 – Library services
Objective

29E.70	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure the preservation of and enhanced access to past, present and future knowledge of the United Nations, to mobilize this knowledge and to increase knowledge-sharing, education and dialogue among Member States and between Member States and the international community.
Strategy
29E.71	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide access to United Nations documents, archives and publications, including both online and paper-based resources, and to external knowledge resources that provide information about the Organization’s areas of work; and guarantee access to paper-based resources stored in the building, and online resources and services;

(b)	Coordinate with the strategic heritage plan to ensure the safe storage of all collections and the accessibility of core collections during the planned closure of the Library building for strategic heritage plan renovations;

(c)	Provide knowledge tools that bring together key information resources and partners on United Nations issues and global agendas, manage the Office’s art collection and digitize and preserve archives and official documents, in collaboration with other United Nations libraries and archives;

(d)	Provide guidance on records management standards and best practices across the Office and at other Secretariat entities in Geneva through the provision of staff training, advisory services and records management services;
(e)	Provide support to other United Nations offices based in Europe requesting storage/archival or library services and solutions;
(f)	Transfer official records of the United Nations, both physical and digital, to the Records Management Unit for long-term preservation;

(g)	Provide support to Member States for cultural diplomacy, connect diplomats with United Nations staff, experts and academic partners through discussion and learning events, and organize learning events and cultural activities, including virtual and blended events.
29E.72	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	More informed participation and subsequent decision-making by diplomats and staff;
(b)	Improved transparency and accountability for the Organization;
(c)	Preservation of institutional and historical memory;
(d)	A vibrant online and on-site cultural activities programme that enables Member States to showcase their art and culture through exhibitions, film screenings and other events;
(e)	Alternative learning opportunities for United Nations staff and diplomats on topics that support career development and the implementation of United Nations policies and agendas.
Programme performance in 2025
Irreplaceable and historic library collections protected for future generations during renovations

29E.73	The Library collections are housed in one of the main buildings of the Palais des Nations, which was constructed in the 1930s and no longer meets current fire and life safety, and building, standards. The building also experienced water intrusions, resulting in damage to and loss of materials. While strategic heritage plan renovations will address these deficiencies, the renovation period itself posed significant risks to print publications, including irreplaceable collections dating back to the sixteenth century. The collection comprises approximately 45,000 shelves and includes United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization Memory of the World Programme documents, historic library holdings and legacy United Nations documents. To mitigate these risks, the subprogramme relocated the entire collection in close coordination with the strategic heritage plan team. The collections were tracked and preservation packed as required, and a temporary reading space was established to provide access to a core collection.
29E.74	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29E.20).
Table 29E.20
Performance measure

table_content.text: Irreplaceable historic collections safeguarded during relocation preparation process
Irreplaceable historic collections are fully protected and secured during renovations, with access to a core collection of library materials

children.text: Result 3: long-term sustainable access to United Nations archives for Member States and other stakeholders

29E.79	The subprogramme is custodian of approximately 15,000 linear metres of United Nations physical records and archives documenting the work of the League of Nations and Secretariat entities in Geneva. Member States and other stakeholders use these archives to analyse and support multilateral cooperation and inform future action. Following the digitization of approximately 14.5 million pages of League of Nations archives, the subprogramme identified that existing storage conditions for physical and digital archives at the Palais pose risks to their long-term preservation, while demand for archival services from other United Nations offices in Europe is increasing. In order to support proper archival storage, the subprogramme in 2023 secured donated funding for a new archive building. In 2024, due diligence and compliance checks, as well as a preliminary feasibility study, were completed.
Lessons learned and planned change

29E.80	The lesson for the subprogramme was that non-specialized storage facilities increase the risk of damage to archival materials and can limit the efficiency of records management and client services. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will construct a purpose-built archives storage facility for the Office and other relevant United Nations offices. The facility will improve preservation, streamline storage and retrieval, and enable faster client access to archives and information services.
